title: HPE Rides The Agentic AI Wave Back Into The Datacenter
gist: HPE is repositioning around agentic AI by moving inference workloads back on-prem, arguing that the per-token economics of continuously-reasoning agents make production AI in the public cloud uneconomical. The piece frames a broader OEM reversal (HPE, Dell, Cisco + Nvidia) of the 'cloud for everything' thesis, with KV-cache storage, agent registries, and Nvidia Vera/Nemotron tooling as the new on-prem AI stack.

insights.claim: HPE cut the cost of running its AI support platform by more than 30x and saved nearly $100,000 a month by moving the AI workloads onto its own on-prem GreenLake Intelligence and Private Cloud AI infrastructure rather than consuming third-party AI.
insights.speaker: Jeff Burt
insights.so_what: A vendor's own 30x cost delta is the clearest data point yet that on-prem inference economics can beat cloud at scale; watch whether enterprise repatriation accelerates and pressures hyperscaler inference revenue.
insights.quote: HPE lowered the cost by more than 30 times, saving nearly $100,000 a month
insights.locator: As a result, HPE lowered the cost...

insights.claim: Agentic AI turns inference into a continuous operational workload rather than a one-time request, because every decision, validation, and action an agent takes consumes a token, causing costs to compound rapidly.
insights.speaker: Jeff Burt
insights.so_what: Reframing inference as a recurring opex line (not a per-query cost) reshapes how to model AI infrastructure demand and per-seat AI software margins.
insights.quote: inference is a continuous operational workload, not a one-time request
insights.locator: Once agents have continuous access to data...

insights.claim: Agents consume 4x to 15x more tokens than standard AI chat interactions, and fully autonomous agents could eventually drive up to 1,000x the inference demand of reasoning AI, per validation firm Signal65.
insights.speaker: Jeff Burt
insights.so_what: A 1,000x inference-demand ceiling is a structural tailwind for inference silicon, power, and on-prem AI hardware vendors; size positions to the agent-adoption curve, not just chat.
insights.quote: autonomous agents could push 1,000 times the inference demand than reasoning AI

insights.claim: A single popular coding-agent deployment processed more than 600 billion tokens in one month to run roughly 100 continuously operating agents, working out to about $13,000 per agent per month.
insights.speaker: Jeff Burt
insights.so_what: $13K/agent/month is a concrete unit-economics anchor for pricing and ROI of agentic software; useful for stress-testing the margins of AI-coding and agent platforms.
insights.quote: comes to about $13,000 per agent per month

insights.claim: HPE's CTO argues that AI economics now resembles infrastructure economics, hinging on utilization, efficiency, and scale across the full system rather than just the model.
insights.speaker: Jeff Burt
insights.so_what: If AI value migrates from model quality to systems efficiency, advantage shifts toward infrastructure operators and integrators over pure model vendors.
insights.quote: Suddenly, AI economics looks a lot like infrastructure economics

insights.claim: Training remains largely cloud-based, but AI inference is moving back on-prem and to the edge, a reversal of the 'cloud for everything' assumption that prevailed two years earlier.
insights.speaker: Jeff Burt
insights.so_what: A train-in-cloud / infer-on-prem split is the key bifurcation to track for hyperscaler vs. OEM revenue mix; favors on-prem hardware and edge AI exposure.
insights.quote: It's moving back on-prem and out to the edge, a quiet reversal that's forcing a fundamental rethink of enterprise AI architecture
insights.locator: Training might happen in the cloud...

insights.claim: A Dell study found that 67 percent of AI workloads run outside the cloud and 88 percent of surveyed organizations run at least one AI workload in their own datacenter.
insights.speaker: Jeff Burt
insights.so_what: Vendor-sourced but striking penetration numbers; if directionally right, on-prem AI is already the majority case, supporting demand for enterprise AI servers and storage.
insights.quote: 67 percent of AI workloads run outside the cloud and 88 percent of those surveyed said they are running at least one AI workload in their own datacenter
insights.locator: founder and chief executive officer Michael Dell pointing to a study...

insights.claim: Traditional OEMs HPE, Dell, and Cisco are all building Nvidia-partnered on-prem 'AI factory' infrastructure and hybrid-management software, converging on the same inference-repatriation strategy.
insights.speaker: Jeff Burt
insights.so_what: Three major OEMs racing the same playbook signals a real market shift and likely margin compression; differentiation will come from software/networking, not boxes.
insights.locator: The trend has driven traditional OEMs...

insights.claim: HPE executives state that for production AI, on-prem is where most enterprise customers are going because the economics of running production in the public cloud don't make sense, while experimentation and training stay in the cloud.
insights.speaker: Jeff Burt
insights.so_what: Confirms the experiment-in-cloud, produce-on-prem pattern as the enterprise default; a watch signal for when AI pilots convert to production and trigger on-prem capex.
insights.quote: The economics don't make sense to be in production in the public cloud.
insights.locator: But when it comes to production...

themes.theme: Storage as active AI memory (KV cache)

insights.claim: HPE positions KV cache offloaded to storage as 'active memory' for AI, turning its Alletra Storage X10000 into a context repository that eliminates rebuilding context and reduces GPU idle time waiting for data.
insights.speaker: Jeff Burt
insights.so_what: KV-cache-to-storage reframes storage as a GPU-efficiency lever; a structural demand driver for high-performance AI storage and a watch item for storage vendors' AI positioning.
insights.quote: KV cache removes the need to rebuild the context, meaning that storage becomes active memory for AI
insights.locator: KV cache removes the need to rebuild the context...

insights.claim: HPE's MX 10000 storage delivered 20x faster time-to-first-token and 17x higher throughput in tests, framing context-memory persistence as a direct contributor to AI economic value.
insights.speaker: Jeff Burt
insights.so_what: Vendor-claimed 20x TTFT gains quantify the storage-efficiency thesis; useful benchmark to validate against independent tests before crediting the AI-storage premium.
insights.quote: the MX 10000 provides 20 times faster time-to-first-token and 17 higher throughput

insights.claim: HPE elevates memory/context from a supply-chain or technical detail to a strategic resource, arguing systems that rebuild context every use waste tokens and slow processes.
insights.speaker: Jeff Burt
insights.so_what: Treating context-memory as strategic reframes where AI cost and competitive advantage sit; watch for memory/caching becoming a distinct product and pricing category.
insights.quote: in AI, memory is no longer a technical detail or a supply chain challenge. It is a strategic resource.

insights.claim: HPE's GreenLake Intelligence adds a central agent registry so organizations can track what agents they have, where they are, and what they are permitted to do, plus OpsRamp Copilot to monitor token-based consumption and cost.
insights.speaker: Jeff Burt
insights.so_what: Agent governance and token-cost observability is an emerging software category; a watch area as enterprises need to control sprawl and spend of autonomous agents.
insights.locator: In software, a focus is on GreenLake Intelligence...

insights.claim: HPE's Private Cloud AI servers will ship with Nvidia's Agent Toolkit (OpenShell secure runtime, NemoClaw blueprints, Nemotron models) to build and orchestrate large-scale multi-agent environments.
insights.speaker: Jeff Burt
insights.so_what: Deepens HPE's dependence on the Nvidia software stack; reinforces Nvidia's platform lock-in extending from silicon into agent orchestration.
insights.locator: Private Cloud AI servers will come with Nvidia's Agent Toolkit...

insights.claim: HPE's ProLiant DL394 Gen12 server, due early next year, will use Nvidia's new Vera CPUs purpose-built to support agents with rapid tool calls, orchestration, and real-time data processing.
insights.speaker: Jeff Burt
insights.so_what: A concrete near-term product milestone tying HPE's agent-server roadmap to Nvidia Vera CPUs; a dated catalyst to watch for the on-prem agentic hardware cycle.
insights.locator: HPE's upcoming ProLiant DL394 Gen12 server...

insights.claim: HPE is cross-pollinating its Aruba branch and campus networking with technology from its $14 billion Juniper Networks acquisition last year, making networking a core part of its AI-datacenter pitch.
insights.speaker: Jeff Burt
insights.so_what: The $14B Juniper deal is being operationalized into AI-infra networking; watch integration execution as networking differentiates OEM AI-factory offerings against Cisco.
insights.locator: HPE also put a focus on its networking...
